attributes.stem: Cancer screening programs have been developed and implemented across Australia over the past 30 years with varying degrees of success. Different age groups are targeted for screening based on cancer risk factors and likelihood of improving mortality.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following screening programs has seen the greatest reduction in malignancy mortality rate?
attributes.explanation:
Principles of a successful screening program include (WHO, 1968):

  1. The condition is an important health problem

  2. There should be a recognisable or early symptomatic stage

  3. The natural history of the condition, including development from latent to declared disease, should be adequately understood

  4. There should be an accepted treatment for patients with recognised disease

  5. There should be a suitable test or examination that has a high level of accuracy

  6. The test should be acceptable to the population

  7. There should be an agreed policy on whom to treat as patients

  8. Facilities for diagnosis and treatment should be available

  9. The cost of screening should be economically balanced in relation to possible expenditure on medical care as a whole

  10. Screening should be a continuing process and not a 'once and for all' project

Breast cancer screening programs were introduced in Australia between 1991 and 1995. BreastScreen Australia targets those aged 50-74 with the aim of identifying suspicious lesions via mammography every two years. Even when accounting for lead-time bias, women diagnosed with breast cancer via screening programs had a 42% lower risk of death compared to those who did not participate in screening.

The first cervical cancer screening program was introduced during 1991 as the Papanicolaou (or Pap) smear and occurring biennially. Pap smears identified dysplastic cells collected from the cervical os. Since 2017, the Cervical Screening Test (CST) program was introduced. Instead of identifying dysplastic cells, the sample is tested for Human Papilloma Virus (HPV). HPV has been implicated in 95% of all cervical cancer diagnoses. Thus early identification of HPV can identify those at risk of, or those already with, cervical cancer. Participation in cervical screening can reduce mortality by 77%. Introduction of the HPV vaccination has also significantly reduced cervical cancer risk by 87% with vaccination received between 12-13 years of age. Thus combined efforts of cervical screening and HPV vaccination have reduced cervical cancer rates and mortality.

Bowel cancer screening was introduced to Australia in 2006 as the immunochemical faecal occult blood test (iFOBT). Eligible participants aged 45-74 years receive screening kits via mail biennially. These tests are able to identify occult blood, thereby indicating the need for further investigation (e.g. colonoscopy). Patients participating in bowel cancer screening had a 40% lower risk of death compared with non-participants.

Prostate cancer screening currently involves measurement of Prostate-specific antigen (PSA). This test has high sensitivity but low specificity for prostate cancer. Causes of elevated PSA include benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), prostatitis and prostate cancer. Significant and sudden increases in PSA, a gradual increase in PSA over time and family history can prompt referral to a urologist for consideration of MRI to identify suspicious lesions.

The development of a successful population level ovarian cancer screening program has not yet occurred. Current investigations include transvaginal ultrasound and measurement of CA-125, an ovarian cancer tumour marker.
learningPoints: Early identification of cancer risk factors (e.g.: HPV, occult blood) or pre-cursour lesions can improve disease survival
references: Wilson JMG, Jungner G. Principles and practices of screening for disease. Geneva, Switzerland: World Health Organization; 1968. Report No.: Public Health Papers No. 34. Available from: http://whqlibdoc.who.int/php/WHO_PHP_34.pdf.
Nkwabong E, Badjan ILB, Sando Z, (2018). Pap smear accuracy for the diagnosis of cervical precancerous lesions. Tropical Doctor, 49 (1): 34-39
Burd EM. (2003). Human Papillomavirus and Cervical Cancer. Clinical Microbiology Reviews, 16 (1): 1-17

Australian Institute of Health and Welfare. Cancer screening [Internet]. Canberra: Australian Institute of Health and Welfare, 2022 [cited 2022 Sep. 25]. Available from: https://www.aihw.gov.au/reports/australias-health/cancer-screening-and-treatment
Australian Institute of Health and Welfare. Analysis of cervical cancer and abnormality outcomes in an era of cervical screening and HPV vaccination in Australia. Canberra: AIHW; 2019.
Neesham D (2007), Ovarian cancer screening. Australian Family Physician, 36 (3)

Merriel SWD, Pocock L, Gilbert E, Creavin S, Walter FM, Spencer A, Hamilton W (2022), Systematic review and meta-analysis of the diagnostic accuracy of prostate-specific antigen (PSA) for the detection of prostate cancer in symptomatic patients. BMC medicine, 20(54)

Xu X, Kharazmi E, Tian Y, Mukama T, Sundquist K, Sundquist J, Brenner H, Fallah M (2021), Risk of prostate cancer in relatives of prostate cancer patients in Sweden: A nationwide cohort study. PLOS Medicine, 18 (6): e1003616
